titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Geoffrey Hartman and Renee Gross Hartman Papers
Geoffrey Hartman and Renee Gross Hartman Papers
titlestmt.author: Compiled by Jessica Quagliaroli and Christy Bailey-Tomecek.

controlnote.p: This encoded finding aid is compliant with the Yale EAD Best Practice Guidelines, Version 1.0.

descriptivenote.p: Finding aid description and metadata in Archives at Yale are licensed under a CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. This means that anyone may copy, modify, and distribute this descriptive metadata without restriction or permission. The CC0 license is explained in full on the Creative Commons website: CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. More information is available on our Help page.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Thursday December 4, 2025 at 03:02

did.unittitle: Geoffrey Hartman and Renee Gross Hartman Papers

descriptivenote.p: The majority of the documents in this collection are in English. The remainder are in French, German, Italian, and Swedish.

did.abstract: The collection covers the personal and professional lives of Geoffrey Hartman and Renee Gross Hartman. Series I. Geoffrey Hartman papers is comprised of seven subseries, which document his publications and work as a Yale faculty member of the Comparative Literature department as well as his involvement establishing the Yale Judaic Studies Program and the Fortunoff Video Archive for Holocaust Testimonies. His personal papers document his education and immigration to the United Kingdom and the United States. Series II. Renee Gross Hartman papers is comprised of five subseries, which document her writings, correspondence, internment at the Bergen-Belsen Concentration Camp, immigration to the United States, and her extensive work with local Jewish organizations in New Haven, Connecticut.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: The collection is open for research.
Box 61 (student, university, and personnel records) is restricted per University policy. For further information contact University Archives at: archives@yale.edu.

Original audiovisual materials, as well as preservation and duplicating masters, may not be played. Researchers must consult use copies, or if none exist must pay for a use copy, which is retained by the repository. Researchers withing to obtain an additional copy of non-commercially produced items for their personal use should consult the ordering reproductions information on the Manuscripts and Archives web site.
Original computer files may not be accessed due to their fragility. Researchers must consult access copies.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: The collection is arranged in two series: Series I. Geoffrey Hartman papers and Series II. Renee Gross Hartman papers. Each series is further arranged into subseries by order of research value.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Geoffrey Hartman (1929-2016) was a literary theorist and a faculty member of the comparative literature department at Yale University. He was part of the "Yale School," a preeminent group of literary theorists and critics whose were influenced by the philosophy of deconstruction. His work focused on the romantic poets (e.g. William Wordsworth, William Blake), literary theory and criticism, and personal narrative, especially as it pertained to Holocaust survivors. Hartman published extensively, both as a sole author and as an editor. Additionally, he was the first faculty advisor for the Fortunoff Video Archive for Holocaust Testimonies and was instrumental in the founding of Yale's Judaic Studies Program. He was born in Frankfurt Germany and was placed in the Kinderstransport to England in 1939. He reunited with his mother in New York in 1945.

Renee Hartman (1933-2025) was born in Bratislava (also called Pressburg in German and Pozsony in Hungarian), in what is today known as the Slovak Republic. She was deported to the Bergen-Belsen concentration camp with her younger sister, Herta (sometimes spelled Hertha), in 1944 when she was ten years old. In 1945, both sisters were liberated and were the surviving members of their immediate family. Renee and Herta came to the US in 1948 by way of Brooklyn and later New Haven, Connecticut. Renee obtained a library science degree from Southern Connecticut State University and worked in public libraries in the New Haven area.

Together, Geoffrey and Renee Hartman were deeply involved in Jewish life at Yale and in New Haven, Connecticut. They helped establish the Fortunoff Video Archive for Holocaust Testimonies and published books of their original poetry.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Geoffrey Hartman and Renee Gross Hartman Papers (MS 2124). Manuscripts and Archives, Yale University Library.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The collection covers the personal and professional lives of Geoffrey Hartman and Renee Gross Hartman.

Series I. Geoffrey Hartman papers is comprised of seven subseries. Subseries I. Writings contains handwritten and typed drafts of Geoffrey Hartman's articles, books, book chapters, essays, lectures, plays, poetry, and reviews, as well as writing fragments.

Subseries II. Fortunoff Video Archive for Holocaust Testimonies (FVAHT) and Holocaust Related Materials contains a significant amount of material related to the establishment of the FVAHT and its early iterations, including the Holocaust Survivors Film Project and the Video Archive project. Also present is correspondence, conference materials, and writings about the Holocaust, as well as organizations such as the Survivors of the Shoah and the Holocaust Memorial Museum.

Subseries III. Professional Papers contains awards, conference materials, teaching and research files, letters of recommendation written by Hartman, and reviews of Hartman's publications. A longtime Yale faculty member, this subseries documents Hartman's work in the Comparative Literature departments, as well as his work with the founding of the Judaic Studies program. Subseries IV. Correspondence traces Hartman's early involvement with the School of Criticism and Theory, the "Yale School" of deconstructionist literary theorists, his professional relationships with other literary theorists, critics, and philosophers, as well as his professional relationships with Jewish studies faculty and scholars. This subseries reflects the close personal and professional relationships that Hartman developed with many of his peers and colleagues.

Subseries V. Personal papers contains family records, photographs, yearbooks, and documents from Hartman's time as a student at Aylesbury Grammar School, Queens College, and Yale University. Family records include immigration papers for both Hartman and his mother, as well as Hartman's name change from Gert Heumann to Geoffrey Hartman.Subseries VI. Audiovisual material includes recordings of lectures on audiocassette tapes, family and travel film recordings, Holocaust survivor testimonies, interviews, and documentaries about the Holocaust. Subseries VII. Restricted contains restricted university, student, and personnel records.

Series II. Renee Gross Hartman papers is comprised of four subseries. Subseries I. Correspondence contains personal and family correspondence. Included is correspondence with the United Restitution Organization, which Gross Hartman worked with to obtain restitution for health issues she and her sister, Herta, developed from being interned at the Bergen-Belsen concentration camp. Series II. Jewish Organizations documents Gross Hartman's extensive work with the Jewish Community Center of New Haven, Connecticut and the New Haven Jewish Federation.

Subseries III. Personal and Professional Papers documents Gross Hartman's education and immigration to the United States, as well as her professional interests in publishing and literature. Subseries IV. Writings contains drafts of Gross Hartman's essays, lectures, poetry, short stories, and writing fragments.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: Copyright has been transferred to Yale University for unpublished materials authored or otherwise produced by the creator(s) of this collection. Copyright status for other collection materials is unknown. Transmission or reproduction of materials protected by U.S. Copyright Law (Title 17, U.S.C.) beyond that allowed by fair use requires the written permission of the copyright owners. Works not in the public domain cannot be commercially exploited without permission of the copyright owners. Responsibility for any use rests exclusively with the user.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: These materials have been arranged and described according to national and local standards. For more information, please refer to the Yale Archival Processing Manual.

The order of the materials has been substantially rearranged by the archivist to promote usability. Original folder titles were retained when available; all other folder titles were supplied by the archivist.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: This series is arranged into seven subseries: Subseries I. Writings, Subseries II. Fortunoff Video Archive for Holocaust Testimonies and Holocaust Related Material, Subseries III. Professional papers, Subseries IV. Correspondence, Subseries V. Personal papers, Subseries VI. Audiovisual Material, Subseries VII. Restricted. Subseries are organized by research value.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series is comprised of seven subseries, which document Geoffrey Hartman's publications, time as a Yale faculty member of the Comparative Literature department, and his involvement in the establishment of the Yale Judaic Studies Program and the Fortunoff Video Archive for Holocaust Testimonies. His personal papers document his education and immigration to the United Kingdom and the United States.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials are grouped alphabetically by publication type: articles, book chapters, books, essays, lectures, plays, poetry, untitled and unidentified writings, and writing fragments. Drafts for individual books are arranged by book chapter order, all other folders are arranged alphabetically within groupings.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries is comprised of printed and handwritten drafts for Geoffrey Hartman's writings, including articles, book chapters, books, essays, lectures, plays, poetry, untitled and unidentified writings, and writing fragments. Also included are notes, reviews, and publishing contracts and agreements. Original chapter draft titles for books have been retained, and researchers should note that these titles may differ from the final, published chapter title. Hartman often used a single piece of writing to produce a lecture, article, and/or book chapter; thus, titles may appear under multiple groupings.

Of note in this series are materials for a Survey of British Literature publication that Hartman proposed to Harcourt, Brace, and World Inc. Publishers. This survey project was never realized, and related documentation to this project can be found in Series IV. Correspondence and Series III. Professional Papers.

Tables of Contents for proposective publications
Fortunoff Video Archive for Holocaust Testimonies and Holocaust related material

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials are arranged alphabetically. Most materials are grouped by topic or document type: conferences and events, correspondence, Fortunoff Video Archive for Holocaust Testimonies, organizations, publications, and writings. Materials within groups are arranged alphabetically.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries contains a significant amount of material related to the establishment of the Fortunoff Video Archive for Holocaust Testimonies (FVAHT) and its early iterations, including the Holocaust Survivors Film Project and the Video Archive project. Also present is correspondence, conference materials, and writings about the Holocaust, as well as organizations such as the Survivors of the Shoah and the Holocaust Memorial Museum. The correspondence in this series documents Geoffrey Hartman's work establishing the FVAHT and his work with other writers, scholars, and organizations on the Holocaust and Jewish studies, such as Elie Wiesel and Fondation Auschwitz.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials are arranged alphabetically. Most materials are grouped by topic or document type: awards and honorary degrees, conferences and events, fellowships, interviews, letters of recommendation, School of Criticism and Theory, teaching and research files, teaching files, travel receipts, writings about Hartman, writings by others, and Yale University.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries contains awards, conference materials, teaching and research files, letters of recommendation written by Geoffrey Hartman, and reviews of his publications. A group of writings by others contains signed articles, essays, and offprints that contain inscriptions and personal messages to Hartman or have been annotated by Hartman.

A longtime Yale faculty member, this subseries documents Hartman's work in the English and Comparative Literature departments, as well as his work with the founding of the Judaic Studies program. The rolodex cards show Hartman's extensive professional network to other literary critics, theorists, and philosophers and Jewish studies faculty and scholars. The rolodex cards can be used to cross-reference with Subseries IV. Correspondence.

Of note are the teaching and research files. These files cover both Hartman's research interests and his teaching materials, which often overlapped. Folders are comprised of a mix of Hartman's research notes, teaching notes, syllabi, course materials, course readings, and reference materials. Because these materials are so intermixed, they have been kept separate from the teaching files group, which does not contain any reference or research material and is comprised of Hartman's administrative files, class notes, notebooks, and syllabi.

did.unittitle: American Academy of Arts and Sciences

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials are arranged alphabetically by last name into two groupings: professional and personal.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries traces Hartman's early involvement with the School of Criticism and Theory, the "Yale School" of deconstructionist literary theorists, his professional relationships with other writers and literary theorists, critics, and philosophers, and his professional relationships with Jewish studies faculty and scholars. This subseries also reflects the personal relationships that Hartman developed with many of his peers and colleagues and there is significant overlap in correspondents in the personal and professional groupings. Hartman's personal correspondence includes letters with his extended family and his mother, Agnes Hartman.

did.unittitle: to Hartman, Agnes, from Geoffrey and Renee Hartman
School of Criticism and Theory at Cornell University
University of California Humanities Research Institute

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries covers Geoffrey Hartman's personal life, from his childhood through adulthood. The family records include immigration papers for both Hartman and his mother, Agnes Hartman, scanned family photographs, a detailed family history, and documents related to Hartman's name change from Gert Heumann to Geoffrey Hartman. The Education records include materials from Hartman's time at Aylesbury Grammar School, his undergraduate work at Queens College, and his graduate work at Yale University.

did.unittitle: Certificate of Merit, Health and Cleanliness Council
New York State Department of Education
Le departement de francais, joue le comedie sur la scene experimentale, du theatre de yale
Hartman family scanned photographs with captions
International Committee on the Red Cross, Application for Correspondence between Sara Hartmann and Gert Heumann
Receipt for payment for two copies of a character reference
Waddesdon Boys and Girls directory and photographs
Midland Daily Telegraph (MDT) Children's Club certificate of membership
Phi Beta Kappa, Sigma Chapter of New York
Slides of artwork by David Newman

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials are arranged alphabetically by topic.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries is comprised of recordings of class lectures on audiocassette tapes, family and travel film recordings, Holocaust Survivor testimonies, interviews, and documentaries about the Holocaust. Interviews include edited and unedited taped interviews for Words & Images, which Geoffrey Hartman co-chaired.

did.unittitle: Die Große Hamburger Straße, Zwischen Toleranz und Terror, ein film von Simone Barck und Kalus Salge
Entret - Ermordet - Vergessen. Judische Schuler in Hamburg
A Jew is Not One Thing
Wie kahn ich singen? Wolf Biermann auf der Suche nach Jizchak Katzenelson
En route to Zurich and London
Words and Images, The Jerusalem Literary Project
"An Idolator at the Altar of Literature," excerpts from the interivew with Cynthia Ozick
Bessie K, Paul D, Leon S
"A Jew, But Not of the Synagogue," excerpts from an interview with Amos Oz
"Settling My Accounts Before I Go Away," excerpts from an interview with Saul Bellow
"Settling My Accounts Before I Go Away," excerpts from an interview with Saul Bellow
Unedited Geoffrey Hartman Interview - The Psalms
"Der Sprechendste Moment 1st Das Schweigen," Radio Feature uber das Fortunoff Video Archive for Holocaust Testimonies an der Yale Universitat
Geoffrey Hartman vs Daniel Goldhagen, Hitler's Willing Executioners
New York State Writers Institute, Humanities 355, Book Show
Sir Frank Kermode with commentary by Geoffrey Hartman
Sir Frank Kermode commentary by J. Guillory et al.
Tommy Cosmo and Bobo demo short
"What's the Word? #9 The Bible as Literature" radio program sponsored by the Modern Language Association of America
United States Holocaust Memorial Museum Witnesses to the Holocaust

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries contains restricted university, student, and personnel records.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Access to this series is restricted per University policy. For further information contact University Archives at: archives@yale.edu.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: This series is arranged into four subseries: Subseries I. Correspondence, Subseries II. Jewish organizations, Subseries III. Personal and professional papers, and Series IV. Writings. Subseries are arranged by research value.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series documents Renee Gross Hartman's personal and professional life, with particular focus on her immigration to the United States, her work with local Jewish organizations in New Haven, Connecticut, and her writings.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials are arranged alphabetically. Original folder titles have been retained.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries contains personal and family correspondence, as well as the United Restitution Organization, which Renee Gross Hartman worked with to obtain restitution for health issues she and her sister, Herta, developed from being interned at the Bergen-Belsen concentration camp. Correspondence with Geoffrey Hartman documents the beginning of their relationship. The personal correspondence grouping includes correspondents found in Series I. Geoffrey Hartman Subseries IV. Correspondence, and documents the professional and personal relationships Gross Hartman developed with Geoffrey Hartman's peers.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries documents Renee Gross Hartman's extensive work with the Jewish Community Center of New Haven, Connecticut and the New Haven Jewish Federation. Materials include Jewish Community Center board member meeting minutes and Gross Hartman's meeting notes, as well as awards from the New Haven Jewish Federation.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials are arranged in two groupings: personal papers and professional papers. Materials are arranged alphabetically within groupings.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries documents Renee Gross Hartman's education and immigration to the United States, as well as her professional interests in publishing and literature.

did.unittitle: Department of Library Service, library training evaluations
Immigration and United States citizenship documents
National Society of Literature and the Arts, membership certificate

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials are grouped alphabetically by publication type: essays, lecture, poetry, and short stories. Materials are arranged alphabetically within groupings.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries contains drafts of Renee Gross Hartman's essays, lectures, poetry, short stories, and writing fragments. Also included are notes and a small number of published copies of her poetry. Gross Hartman's writing focuses on themes of self-reflection, identity, and motherhood, and draws on her experience while interned at the Bergen-Belsen concentration camp during the Holocaust.

did.unittitle: "My Life as a Man; or, When Boyhood Was in Forever," draft
Stories in the Hand, prospective publication
Talent , vol. 5, New Haven State Teachers College
"The Woman at Yale: A Response and Minority Report"
